title: Security Metrics for Nonlinear Optical Light Sources from Interferometric Field Reconstruction
authors: Zijian Gan, Shuyue Feng, Camryn J. Gloor, Wei You, Andrew M. Moran
abstract: Nonlinear optical light sources enable the generation of photons with quantum states that are intrinsically linked to underlying material dynamics, rather than imposed through external modulation. Here we investigate fundamental quantum communication metrics of four-wave-mixing signal fields generated by the two-dimensional perovskite (PEA)2PbI4. Using polarization-resolved interferometric measurements together with a microscopic nonlinear response model for the Bloch vector, we reconstruct effective single-photon polarization density matrices inferred from the experimental signal fields and evaluate the corresponding Holevo bound and effective secret-bit rates as a function of the coherence time, population time, and detection wavelength. We find that incorporating the coherence-time degree of freedom systematically lowers the Holevo bound by approximately 2.6-5.8% across the various excitonic resonances, indicating reduced distinguishability of the polarization states when the full multidimensional parameter space is sampled. To connect the polarization-state indistinguishability with experimentally achievable throughput, we further introduce an effective secret-bits-per-pulse metric that enables rapid evaluation of secure information throughput for candidate materials without requiring photon-number-resolved detection. For the present system, control of the population time via spin-dependent evolution yields substantially higher secret-bit rates than manipulation of the coherence time, while spectral regions associated with single-exciton and biexciton resonances define complementary operating regimes for secure communication. More broadly, this work positions nonlinear spectroscopy as a framework for exploring how emergent optical materials can generate and structure quantum states in ways that are advantageous for established quantum communication schemes.
summary: This paper investigates how the intrinsic spin and coherence dynamics of 2D perovskites can be used to generate secure quantum communication signals. By analyzing the Holevo bound and secret-bit rates, the authors show that controlling material parameters like population time can optimize information throughput. The work establishes a framework for using nonlinear spectroscopy to design new materials for quantum key distribution.
main_problem: Evaluating the security metrics (Holevo bound and secret-bit rates) of quantum communication using nonlinear optical light sources generated from material-intrinsic dynamics.
main_result: Incorporating coherence time into the parameter space reduces the Holevo bound by 2.6-5.8%, and population-time encoding is found to be significantly more efficient for high-throughput communication than coherence-time encoding.
method: The researchers use polarization-resolved interferometric measurements and field reconstruction to extract polarization density matrices, combined with a microscopic nonlinear response model for the Bloch vector.
model_or_system: The physical system is a 2D organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite ((PEA)2PbI4) utilizing four-wave-mixing (FWM) signal fields, modeled via a reduced-order response-function framework involving single-exciton and biexciton resonances.
key_observables: Polarization contrast (C_pol), relative phase (delta_phi), Holevo bound (chi), effective secret-bit yield (Y_eff), and Bloch vector components.
important_parameters: Coherence time (tau), population/spin-relaxation time (T), detection wavelength (lambda), and spectral overlap (Xi_X,XXB).
main_assumptions: The calculation assumes reconstructed fields are pure by construction, the system operates in the slow-modulation limit, and the exciton fine-structure splittings are smaller than the spectroscopic linewidths.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the FWM communication approach and spin dynamics; Fig 2 details the experimental setup; Fig 3 displays energy levels and transient absorption; Fig 4 shows polarization components and contrast; Fig 5 shows polarization modulation; Fig 6 presents Bloch-sphere representations; Fig 7 compares encoding efficiencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quantum communication problem, describes the experimental FWM setup and material properties, presents the field reconstruction and security metric derivation, analyzes the results across different spectral regimes and encoding strategies, and concludes with implications for material-intrinsic quantum engineering.
why_interesting: It bridges nonlinear spectroscopy with quantum information theory, demonstrating how many-body dynamics (exciton-biexciton interactions) and spin relaxation can be engineered to optimize the security and throughput of photonic quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Quantum communication can be implemented through a material-intrinsic approach in which information is encoded in the polarization states of four-wave-mixing sig- nal photons. Spin relaxation occurs during the pump–probe delay time T, whereas polarization dynamics associated with the coherence time between pump pulses, τ, are largely unex- plored. (b) Absorption of circularly polarized light initiates spin-relaxation processes in which exciton populations equili- brate within the fine structure of 2D-OIHP quantum wells on a 100 fs timescale.

FIG. 2. (a) Four-wave mixing experiments were conducted with a diffractive-optic–based interferometer, in which the delay time between the first two pulses, τ, is varied by translating thin fused-silica prism wedges. The initial pump pulse pair was circularly polarized, whereas the third (probe) pulse was vertically polarized. The vertical and horizontal polarization components of the signal pulses are split into two spatially separated beams with equal intensities using a beam-displacing polarizer and are then simultaneously detected by spectral interferometry using a Czerny–Turner spectrograph and CMOS camera. (b) The first two laser pulses are separated by the coherence delay τ, and the...

FIG. 3. (a) Three nearly degenerate single-exciton states orig- inate from the coupling between electron and hole angular momenta, where the total angular momentum quantum num- bers are denoted MJ = −1, 0, +1. The excitonic basis also includes three biexciton states, which are energetically shifted relative to the single-exciton manifold because of angular mo- mentum interactions between the constituent excitons. (b) Single-exciton and biexciton resonances exhibit distinct sen- sitivities to circularly polarized laser excitation in transient absorption experiments. Signals acquired using the same and opposite circularly polarized pump–probe configurations dif- fer at time zero due to the...

FIG. 4. (a)–(d) Vertical and horizontal polarization components of the transient grating signal are measured using left-circularly polarized pump pulses and a vertically polarized probe. The population time is denoted by T, while the coherence time τ is fixed at zero. (e) The polarization contrast in the signal field Cpol(τ, T, λ) varies among the single-exciton and biexciton resonances. (f)–(h) Normalized signal waveforms are extracted from the experimental data at a detection wavelength of 535 nm. Spin relaxation induces an elliptical-to-linear transformation of the signal field polarization.

FIG. 5. The (a)-(b) vertical and (c)-(d) horizontal polarization components of the transient grating signal field are measured at T = 0 while scanning the coherence delay time τ between the pump pulses. The indices of the LLVV and LLVH tensor elements follow a successive ordering, indicating that the pump pulses are left-circularly polarized and the probe pulse is vertically polarized. Polarization contrast is measured while scanning both the coherence time (τ) and population time (T), revealing periodic modulations in spectral regions associated with the single-exciton and lower-energy biexciton resonances.
